problem_summary: 2019 MCM Problem C (data): characterize the spread/characteristics of synthetic opioid and heroin drug-identification reports across ~460+ counties in 5 states (OH, KY, WV, VA, PA) over 2010-2017 using DEA/NFLIS data; identify likely origin locations of specific opioids; find significant U.S. Census socio-economic factors driving opioid use; and propose plus quantitatively validate a counter-strategy. Team 1922154 (Outstanding) reframed this not as a regression/time-series forecasting problem but as a spatial-spread simulation problem, building a custom Cellular Automata model whose transition rule is learned from history via clustering + KNN + inverse-distance weighting, then folding Census factors into the CA state vector and using the same model to stress-test policy actions.
models_used: Cellular Automata (CA) spatial-spread model: each county = one cell with attributes (occupancy flag c, current case count C_i, yearly growth rate r_i); map of 5 states discretized into cells, state updated year-by-year from 2010 initial state out to 2026

Data-driven CA transition rule built from clustering + KNN: each county + its k nearest counties (by geographic distance) form an 'environment' vector; 3003 historical environment vectors generated across county-years
K-Nearest-Neighbors matching in environment space: find the m most-similar historical environments by Euclidean distance to infer a county's next-year growth rate
Inverse Distance Weighting (IDW): blend the r values of the m matched historical environments, weighted by 1/d, to produce the county's growth rate r_i = sum(w_j * r_j)
Haversine formula: compute great-circle distances between county lat/long pairs to define spatial neighborhoods

Concentration Index (CI): a Herfindahl-Hirschman-Index (HHI) analogue, CI = sum((C_i / sum C_i)^2) over counties in a state, to quantify spatial concentration vs. diffusion of cases per state per year
One-way ANOVA: significance screening of Census socio-economic attributes by comparing top-25% vs bottom-25% case-count county groups (used when variance-homogeneity test passes)
Pearson correlation analysis: secondary/alternative screen for attributes failing the variance-homogeneity test, and to assign direction of effect

Modified CA model: significant Census factors (marital status MS, educational attainment EA, ancestry AN, language spoken LS) injected into the environment vector via reciprocal transforms (e.g. MS = 1/Mn + 1/Fn) so small percentages are not drowned out in the Euclidean distance
RMSE-based grid search over (k, m) hyperparameters for model selection and sensitivity analysis (original optimum k=12, m=2, RMSE 104.21; modified optimum k=5, m=2, RMSE 98.70)
Threshold/'concern' detection: per-state drug-identification threshold levels (e.g. Ohio 120,000) derived from historical ceilings to flag when/where government concern is triggered (Ohio in 2026)

Control-variates policy simulation: perturb a single factor (MS up 5-20%, or Lo down 5-20%) and re-run the CA to quantify case reduction (257,496 -> 231,073 for marriage incentive; -> 225,873 for English training)
key_techniques: Reframing a 'data' problem as a spatial-temporal simulation rather than a regression: the CA captures county-to-county contagion and self-history, directly answering the 'spread between counties over time' and 'where did it start' sub-questions that a county-by-county regression cannot

Learning the CA transition rule from data instead of hand-coding it: rather than an arbitrary physics-style rule, the growth rate comes from analogically matching the current local environment to historically observed environments (KNN + IDW) — a clever fusion of CA + instance-based learning

Ascending-sort of neighbor counts inside the environment vector before computing Euclidean distance, so two neighborhoods with the same multiset of values aren't treated as dissimilar due to ordering — a small but well-justified invariance fix that reduces matching error

Borrowing HHI from economics and repurposing it as an opioid Concentration Index — a defensible, citable, single-number diffusion metric that turns 'is it spreading or concentrating' into a plottable time series per state

Reciprocal feature transform (1/Mn, 1/Ph, etc.) to rescale tiny Census percentages so they actually influence the Euclidean-distance-based neighbor matching — addresses a real feature-scaling pitfall head-on

Two-stage statistical filter for factor selection: ANOVA on extreme quartile groups for initial screening, then Pearson correlation as a second gate, with an honest discussion of why the two disagree (local-vs-global sample size effect)
RMSE heatmap over the full (k,m) grid serving double duty as both hyperparameter selection AND sensitivity analysis, with results tabulated exhaustively in the appendix
Closing the loop: the SAME validated model is reused to quantify the proposed policy's effect via control-variates perturbation, giving concrete before/after numbers rather than hand-waving
why_it_won: "From a judge's standpoint the paper wins on a single strong, original idea executed end-to-end. (1) Methodological novelty with justification: it does not reach for an off-the-shelf SIR/ARIMA/regression; it invents a CA whose rule is mined from data via clustering+KNN+IDW, and explicitly motivates each choice against the problem's three stated challenges (interaction between counties, influence of own history, simulate all counties). (2) It answers every prompt sub-question concretely — spread maps 2017-2026, named origin/aggregation locations (transportation hubs, Lexington KY for heroin), significant factors (MS/EA/AN/LS), and a strategy. (3) It quantifies everything: CI time series, RMSE grid, threshold levels, and policy before/after case counts (257,496 -> 231,073 / 225,873). (4) It demonstrates genuine self-critique — the ANOVA-vs-correlation disagreement is explained rather than hidden, and weaknesses (long-horizon error, overfitting on small validation data, ignoring non-NFLIS counties) are stated plainly. (5) The model is shown to be improved by the socio-economic data (RMSE 104.21 -> 98.70), tying the two halves of the problem into one coherent, extensible framework rather than two disconnected analyses. That integration + originality + quantified validation is exactly the Outstanding signature."
section_highlights: "Strong structural choices: (a) A one-page summary plus a separate formatted memo 'To: Chief Administrator' that front-loads the two concrete policy actions and their numeric impact — gives the judge the punchline before the math. (b) An explicit 'Planned Approach' with a framework flowchart (Figure 1) mapping data -> CA model -> ANOVA/correlation -> strategy, so the whole pipeline is visible up front. (c) Dedicated Terminology/Symbols/Assumptions section with each assumption paired to an explicit reason. (d) Heavy, purposeful visualization: heatmaps of case distribution by year, CI line charts per state, and RMSE heatmaps — every figure answers a question. (e) Clean separation into a baseline CA (Section 3) and a socio-economically-modified CA (Section 4), letting the improvement be measured rather than asserted. (f) Honest Strength/Weakness section and an appendix dumping the full (k,m) RMSE table for reproducibility. The narrative consistently moves prompt-question -> method -> formula -> figure -> interpretation, which is easy for a judge to follow and grade."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Self-admitted long-horizon unreliability: predictions to 2026 rest on Assumption 2 (no major government intervention, dynamics follow 2010-2017 law) — the team concedes 1-2 year error is small but long-term error is large, yet still reports 2026 threshold conclusions (e.g. Ohio hits 120,000) as if actionable

Overfitting risk acknowledged but not mitigated: the KNN/IDW rule is fit and validated on only 7 years of data; with so few real points to validate against, the chosen (k,m) optimum may not generalize, and the modified model's RMSE improvement (104.21 -> 98.70, ~5%) is marginal and could be noise

Closed-system spatial bias: Assumption 4 drops all counties not in NFLIS data, so the model cannot represent spread INTO clean counties — a serious limitation for a contagion model, which they note as a weakness

Causal over-reach in the socio-economic story: positive correlations (e.g. bachelor's-degree share, specific ancestries Arab/Greek/Irish/Italian/Russian/Ukrainian, never-married share) are interpreted with speculative, borderline-stereotyping causal narratives (e.g. immigrants as 'unstable factors' enabling trafficking) — correlation is treated as mechanism without support, a real scientific and ethical weak point

Policy actions are weakly grounded: 'tax/mortgage discounts to encourage marriage' and 'cheap English classes' are inferred purely from correlational factors, and the validation perturbs a model variable rather than demonstrating any real-world causal lever; the r=5% / r=-10% rows even produce simulation > actual (non-monotone), which the team waves off as 'error'
The reciprocal transforms (1/Mn etc.) are an ad hoc scaling hack with no principled normalization (e.g. z-scoring would be cleaner) and could create instability when a percentage is near zero

OCR-degraded artifact: in the archived markdown the figures' underlying data tables are corrupted/garbled (e.g. the 2010 'heatmap' table, late-year bar-chart values like '142','8','4','2'), so quantitative figure values cannot all be trusted from this copy — a corpus/provenance caveat, not a flaw of the original paper
reusable_patterns: Data-driven Cellular Automata template: discretize space into cells, define a small attribute set per cell, and LEARN the transition rule from history via KNN over 'environment' vectors + inverse-distance weighting instead of hand-coding it — reusable for any spatial-spread 'data' problem (disease, adoption, crime, invasive species)

The 'environment vector' = (target growth rate, self value, sorted neighbor values, optional covariates) construction, with ascending-sort to enforce permutation invariance before Euclidean matching — a clean, transferable feature-engineering trick for neighborhood similarity
Repurpose an economics concentration metric (HHI -> Concentration Index) as a single-number, citable spatial diffusion indicator plotted over time per region

Two-stage factor selection: ANOVA on extreme quartile groups for screening, then correlation as a confirmation gate, plus an explicit explanation when they disagree (local significance washing out at full-sample scale) — a rigorous, defensible variable-selection workflow
RMSE heatmap over a 2D hyperparameter grid (k vs m) serving simultaneously as model selection AND sensitivity analysis, with the full table in an appendix for reproducibility
Reciprocal/feature-rescaling step so small-magnitude covariates aren't ignored by distance-based methods — generalizable lesson: always check feature scale before any Euclidean/KNN step

Close-the-loop validation: reuse the SAME fitted model to simulate a policy via control variates (perturb one factor, hold others fixed) and report concrete before/after numbers — turns 'recommend a strategy' into a quantified claim

Reader-first packaging: summary + standalone decision-maker memo that states the recommended actions and their numeric impact before any modeling detail; pair every assumption with its reason; lead each subsection with the prompt question it answers
Honest Strength/Weakness section that names overfitting, horizon error, and closed-system bias explicitly — judges reward calibrated self-criticism over false confidence
